retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Amniotic Fluid -- Development -- Late Gestation
retrieved_docs.content: Once the coelomic fluid begins to disappear, the amniotic cavity takes over. In the early stages of gestation, the water in amniotic fluid is derived mostly from maternal serum; however, at 10 weeks, the fetus begins to produce urine which is secreted into the amniotic sac. During late gestation (the second and third trimesters), as the amniotic fluid expands, fetal urine becomes the largest source of the amniotic fluid. [6] Lung secretions, gastrointestinal secretions, and excretions from the umbilical cord and placental surface contribute to the composition of amniotic fluid as well; however, lung secretions alone make up as much as one-third of amniotic fluid. [4] [7] Compared to the composition of yellow coelomic fluid early in pregnancy, amniotic fluid is less viscous and always clear due to its lower protein concentration. Amniotic fluid is 98% water and electrolytes, and signaling molecules, peptides, carbohydrates, lipids, and hormones make up the other 2%. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Amniotic Fluid -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Homeostasis of body fluids is important in the growing fetus. In addition to the constant circulation of amniotic fluid through inhalation and exhalation, there must be a balance between fluid formation and elimination. [4] The formation originates from fetal urine and lung secretions; however, elimination which is important for balance and homeostasis, is largely the result of fetal swallowing and intramembranous absorption. [7] [8] Early in pregnancy, embryonic skin is just simple epithelium, allowing fluid to pass freely under hydrostatic and osmotic forces. [3] Furthermore, its composition is similar to the fetus and maternal serum; it freely diffuses through fetal skin and the chorionic villi until week 8. Eventually, fetal skin begins to become stratified epithelium and becomes fully keratinized by 25 weeks. [4] [9] Once the skin of the fetus is fully keratinized later in pregnancy, it can no longer absorb or transfer fluids as easily back and forth. Respiration, swallowing, and urination are the main routes of exchange between the fetus and amniotic fluid to maintain the fluid balance. [3] The two largest contributors to elimination are fetal swallowing and the intramembranous pathway. Though there are many mechanisms for eliminating amniotic fluid, the greatest contributor to amniotic fluid elimination is through fetal swallowing, seen as early as 11 weeks. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Evaluation of Amniotic Fluid -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Amniotic fluid Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: At the beginning of pregnancy, amniotic fluid is primarily a byproduct of maternal serum and resembles plasma due to hydrostatic and osmotic pressures. After 8 weeks of gestation, the fetal kidneys begin to function and produce urine; however, this does not become a key component of amniotic fluid until closer to 23 to 25 weeks, when fetal keratinization is complete. [1] By the time keratinization is complete, fetal urinary production accounts for the most significant amount of amitotic fluid, with up to 1500 mL produced daily. [15] In the last 20 weeks, fetal respiratory and gastric secretions also contribute up to 350 mL per day. Amniotic fluid is recycled primarily by fetal swallowing. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniocentesis -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Physiology of Amniotic Fluid
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal compartments are essential in forming amniotic fluid in the first trimester of pregnancy. Fetal skin is non-keratinized in earlier weeks, allowing free transfer of water and other small molecules and solutes through the amnion and chorionic. [8] Amniotic fluid is similar to maternal and fetal extracellular fluid and functions as a nonsterile aqueous electrolyte solution. During the second trimester of pregnancy, the diffusion process ceases as keratinization of fetal skin occurs, making the fetal skin impervious to water and other solutes.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Amniotic Fluid -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The development of amniotic fluid organizes into early gestation and late gestation. Early gestation is the embryonic period which is from the start of fertilization to 8 weeks, and late gestation, which encompasses the fetal period 8 weeks to birth.  The composition of amniotic fluid changes from early gestation to late gestation. During the embryonic period, amniotic fluid derives from both fetal and maternal factors such as water from maternal serum, coelomic fluid, and fluid from the amniotic cavity; however, during late gestation, amniotic fluid is largely produced by fetal urine and lung secretions. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Oligohydramnios -- Etiology -- Amniotic fluid production
retrieved_docs.content: In the first trimester of pregnancy, lung secretions and hydrostatic and osmotic transport of maternal plasma through the fetal membranes comprise the majority of amniotic fluid production. Between 14 and 16 weeks gestation, the fetal kidneys begin to produce urine, which becomes the primary source of amniotic fluid for the remainder of the gestation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Fluid Index(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Throughout intrauterine development, the fetus exists in a compartment filled with amniotic fluid, allowing free movement. [1] This fluid acts as a protective cushion against potential injuries, helps maintain a stable temperature, and is inhaled and swallowed by the fetus, contributing to normal lung development. [2] Amniotic fluid is a dynamic substance, with its volume and chemical makeup regulated within narrow limits. [3] The amount of amniotic fluid gradually increases until approximately 34 weeks of gestation, after which it slightly decreases until the 40th week and then declines more sharply until the 42nd week. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Evaluation of Amniotic Fluid -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the early stages of pregnancy, the composition of amniotic fluid resembles a complex dialysate derived from maternal serum. As the fetus grows, changes occur in the fluid's composition; notably, sodium concentration and osmolality decrease while urea, creatinine, and uric acid levels increase. [4] [5] Amniotic fluid also contains various steroid and protein hormones. [6] [7] Initially, it has little to no particulate matter, but by 16 weeks gestation, a significant number of cells shed from the amnion, skin, and tracheobronchial tree are present. These cells are crucial for antenatal diagnosis and serve as a source of DNA for karyotype analysis following amniocentesis. [8] Typically, fetuses do not defecate during pregnancy; however, if under severe stress, they may pass meconium. This material contains bile pigments that can stain the amniotic fluid green, indicating fetal stress. [9] The regulation of amniotic fluid volume (AFV) involves 3 primary mechanisms: placental control of water and solute transfer, fetal contributions through urine production and swallowing, and maternal factors affecting fluid balance.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Amniotic Fluid -- Development -- Early Gestation
retrieved_docs.content: In early gestation, two fluid-filled sacs surround the embryo: the exocoelomic cavity and the amniotic cavity. The formation of the coelomic cavity begins during the fourth week of gestation when the exocoelomic cavity splits the extraembryonic mesoderm into the splanchnic mesoderm lining and the somatic mesoderm. The coelomic fluid within the coelomic cavity stays in direct contact with the mesenchyme of the developing placenta villi during the first trimester. Before it disappears, the coelomic cavity acts as a transfer area as well as a reservoir of nutrients for the growing embryo. The exocoelomic cavity forms inside the extraembryonic mesoderm alongside the placental chorionic plate and is now believed to be an essential transfer interface and reservoir of nutrients for the embryo because coelomic fluid has shown to have ultrafiltrate of maternal serum as well as products derived from the placenta and secondary yolk sac. This arrangement suggests that the coelomic fluid is essentially an extension of the placenta, providing the embryo with nutrients until the amniotic cavity becomes large enough to take over later in development. [2] [5] Gradually, the coelomic cavity shrinks as the amniotic cavity expands and completely disappears by week 12. [5] [6] At this point in development, the primary function of amniotic fluid is the expansion of the amniotic sac, which allows room for the fetus to grow unimpeded. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Amniotic Fluid -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic fluid surrounds the embryo and fetus during development and has a myriad of functions. Physically, it protects the fetus in the event the maternal abdomen is the object of trauma. Furthermore, it protects the umbilical cord by providing a cushion between the fetus and the umbilical cord, thus reducing the risk of compression between the fetus and the uterine wall. [1] Amniotic fluid also helps protect the fetus from infectious agents due to its inherent antibacterial properties. Additionally, it serves as a reservoir of fluid and nutrients for the fetus containing: proteins, electrolytes, immunoglobulins, and vitamins from the mother. It provides the necessary fluid, space, and growth factors to allow normal development and growth of fetal organs such as the musculoskeletal system, gastrointestinal system, and pulmonary system. [2] Clinicians can use amniotic fluid as a tool to monitor the progression of pregnancy and predict fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Fluid Index(Archived) -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure -- Alternative Measurements of Amniotic Fluid Volume
retrieved_docs.content: A single deepest pocket is used in pregnancies less than 24 weeks or with multiple gestations. [28] The technique used to measure a single deepest pocket, also called a maximum vertical pocket, is identical to the measurement of amniotic fluid amounts in the 4 quadrants used to determine an amniotic fluid index. The entire uterus should be examined, and the single deepest vertical pocket of fluid should be identified and measured. A normal single deepest pocket is 2 to 8 cm. A measurement less than 2 cm indicates oligohydramnios, whereas a measurement greater than 8 cm is polyhydramnios. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Fluid Index(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic fluid also contains various steroid and protein hormones. [7] [8] Initially, it has little to no particulate matter, but by 16 weeks of gestation, a significant number of cells shed from the amnion, skin, and tracheobronchial tree are present. These cells are crucial for antenatal diagnosis and serve as a source of DNA for karyotype analysis following amniocentesis. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Fluid Index(Archived) -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Decreased fetal urine output can arise from several causes, which fall into 2 general categories—fetal urinary tract obstruction and decreased urine production by the fetal kidney. [15] Urinary tract obstruction can occur anywhere along the fetal urinary tract and can be catastrophic for the fetus. Decreased urine production by the fetal kidney typically reflects inadequate blood flow to the fetal kidney, caused by the shunting of fetal blood flow away from the kidney to the heart and brain. This mechanism mirrors the cause of oliguria in critically ill adults. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniocentesis -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic fluid is a transparent fluid with a light yellowish color. It is present in the amniotic sac. It creates a space for the fetus to grow and survive. It helps permit fetal movements, which are necessary for the effective musculoskeletal development of the fetus. It helps in fetal swallowing and fetal breathing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2420
retrieved_docs.content: Amnioinfusion and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية (chorionic villus sampling) are both categorized as Antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures. These procedures are performed before delivery and involve the insertion of instruments or substances into the maternal‑fetal environment for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. Amnioinfusion specifically refers to the infusion of fluid into the amniotic cavity to alleviate conditions such as oligohydramnios or improve fetal monitoring, while خزعة الزغابات المشيمية involves the sampling of placental tissue to obtain fetal genetic material for early prenatal diagnosis. Both interventions are considered child procedures within the broader class of antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures, highlighting their role in prenatal care and fetal assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotomy at delivery is classified as an obstetrics incision, representing a specific procedural approach used to artificially rupture the membranes during labor. The broader category of obstetrics incision encompasses additional child procedures, including البزل القلبي (cardiac puncture) and بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniotic fluid tap). Together, these interventions illustrate the range of techniques that fall under obstetric incisions, highlighting their roles in managing both maternal and fetal conditions during pregnancy and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
retrieved_docs.content: Amnioinfusion, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي, and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية are all classified under the category of antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures. These interventions are performed before delivery to modify, sample, or assess the amniotic environment. Amnioinfusion involves the introduction of fluid into the amniotic cavity, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniocentesis) entails aspiration of amniotic fluid for diagnostic testing, and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية (chorionic villus sampling) involves obtaining placental tissue for genetic analysis. Recognizing that these procedures share a common therapeutic and diagnostic purpose helps clinicians select appropriate antepartum interventions and coordinate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1602
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics incision denotes a surgical opening employed in obstetric practice, and it encompasses specific procedures such as البزل القلبي (cardiac puncture) and بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniotic fluid puncture). Both البزل القلبي and بزل السائل الأمنيوسي are specialized techniques performed under the umbrella of obstetrics incision to obtain diagnostic or therapeutic access to fetal cardiac structures or amniotic fluid, respectively, and are integral components of advanced obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics incision is a broad category that includes several specific procedures used in pregnancy and childbirth, namely البزل القلبي, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي, and بضع الفرج. البزل القلبي refers to a cardiac puncture performed in obstetric contexts, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي denotes amniocentesis for sampling amniotic fluid, and بضع الفرج describes the vaginal delivery process. Recognizing that these three distinct techniques are all considered children of the obstetrics incision concept helps clinicians understand the range of incisional or puncture methods available for diagnostic and therapeutic management in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 218
retrieved_docs.content: as a child entity, reflecting the impact of maternal health on the fetus. Late effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications (التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس) are themselves classified as pregnancy complications, underscoring the long‑term sequelae that can follow obstetric events. Amniotic cavity and membrane problems are classified as polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity, which in turn are categorized as abnormal amniocentesis (البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي). This classification highlights that excessive amniotic fluid and related membrane abnormalities are often identified through abnormal amniotic fluid sampling. Polyhydramnios and other problems of the amniotic cavity also have child conditions such as premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) and infection in the amniotic cavity (عدوى في الجوف السلوي), indicating a hierarchical relationship where fluid overload can lead to membrane rupture and subsequent infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases serve as clinically relevant resources for a range of obstetric conditions. They are linked to the study and management of الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (inflammation of the placenta and membranes), حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (a specific type of abnormal pregnancy), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (retained products of conception), and gestation twin (twin pregnancy). By aggregating data on these entities, databases support diagnosis, risk assessment, and therapeutic decision‑making in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: Intervillous hemorrhage of placenta is a disorder characterized by bleeding into the placental intervillous space and is regarded as a condition affecting the fetal membranes or amniotic fluid; it occurs in the context of a pregnancy in which a child is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is classified as Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity. The category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity includes specific obstetric conditions such as تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes) and عدوى في الجوف السلوي (infection of the amniotic cavity). This grouping indicates that disorders involving abnormal amniotic fluid volume, membrane integrity, and intra‑amniotic infection are interrelated and considered subtypes of the broader polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity problems, guiding clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of amniotic cavity and membrane disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2442
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem is classified as belonging to the diagnostic category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity. The category Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is used to group and define conditions such as polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity abnormalities, providing a framework for clinicians to identify, assess, and manage these specific obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 763
retrieved_docs.content: and risk factors of pre‑eclampsia, placental disorders, amniotic‑fluid deficiencies, uterine contractile events, and labor‑induction failures with both uncomplicated (normal) and high‑risk obstetric scenarios, including singleton and multiple gestations. Recognizing these interrelationships supports more precise risk stratification, diagnosis, and management of pregnant patients across the full spectrum of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2215
retrieved_docs.content: Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is a diagnostic category that groups together various disorders affecting the amniotic cavity, and it specifically includes the condition تمزق الأغشية الباكر (early membrane rupture) as a child entity within this classification. By classifying polyhydramnios and related amniotic cavity abnormalities under a single umbrella, clinicians can recognize that early membrane rupture is considered part of the broader spectrum of amniotic cavity problems, which aids in comprehensive obstetric evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: structural anomalies, amniotic‑fluid disturbances, placental pathology and multiple gestations to provide clinicians with a comprehensive classification system for risk assessment, monitoring and intervention planning in obstetric care, while also distinguishing normal delivery pathways and related sequelae such as vesicovaginal fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity is presented as a diagnostic category that includes a range of fetal and maternal complications, and within this category the specific condition عدوى في الجوف السلوي (infection of the pleural cavity) is identified as a child entity, indicating that infection of the pleural cavity may be considered a related or co‑occurring problem in the context of amniotic cavity disorders. The category also classifies itself under the label polyhydramnios and other problems of amniotic cavity, reinforcing its definition as a distinct group of amniotic fluid abnormalities and associated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: and abnormal forces of labor, illustrating how fetal factors can influence maternal care. Overall, the framework links broad diagnostic categories to precise maternal and fetal conditions, supporting accurate coding, clinical awareness, and patient management.
